title: crlf.response_splitting
description: CRLF / HTTP response splitting proven by an engine-owned nonce becoming a NEW parsed response header (header-line injection) in the candidate but not the benign control.
x-notes: The engine builds the candidate and control arms by planting each payloads value into injection.location of base_request; the arms differ only in that one slot.

The split payload MUST contain the {{nonce}} slot AND construct a new header line via CR LF, e.g. "en\r\nX-Nocap-Split-{{nonce}}: 1". Per run the engine substitutes {{nonce}} with a fresh 128-bit value and requires a PARSED candidate response header whose name carries that nonce (or whose value equals it) — and which is absent from the control. Body reflection cannot fake this: the signal is read from the response header set, not the body. Header folding cannot fake it either: a folded value never produces a new header name.

Use kind=query|form|json_body|json_operator. The header and url_token kinds reject CR/LF by design, so a CRLF payload there yields invalid — response splitting is proven through a reflected parameter, not a guarded request header.

This proves a response-header-injection primitive is reachable (an attacker controls a header the parser honors). It does not by itself prove cache poisoning, session fixation, or XSS via the split — record any downstream impact as a separate PoC-level claim.
